coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence sensor based on BSA modified sulfur nanosheets for sequential detection Fe3+ and salicylic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shan
Shen, Lihua
Qiang, Tao
Xiao, Jianyi
Cai, Chenyun
Jia, Shibo
Zhang, Xijing
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur nanosheets (S-NSs) have received increasing attention in biochemistry fielded due to adequate inherent antibacterial, antifungal activities, outstanding stability and reusability. The electron transfer between the iron ions (Fe3+) and S-NSs brings S-NSs aggregation lead to the fluorescence intensity of S-NSs decreases in a wide linear concentration range from 12 to 108 μM. More interestingly, the quenched fluorescence of S-NSs/Fe3+ could be recovered by salicylic acid (SA) in a linear range of 8–80 μM because of a stable complex could be form between SA and Fe3+and the proposed sensor for SA showed the outstanding advantages of simplicity, specificity, and fast. The method included environmentally friendly, low-cost, convenient, and wide linear range, which makes it a novel method for routine applications for Fe3+ and SA detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur nanosheets
Fe3+
Salicylic acid
Fluorescence
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of the reaction time of S-NSs with Fe3+
Detection of Fe3+ ions based on S-NSs
Detection of SA based on S-NSs/Fe3+ system
Exploration of the S-NS sensing properties for Fe3+ and salicylic acid

The Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Biological Analysis of the Ministry of Education of Fuzhou University (FS2205). Blue Talent Project of Xianyang Normal University (XSYQL 202002).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of S nanosheets (a, TEM of S-SNs; b, HR-TEM of S-SNs; c, AFM of S-SNs;d, XPS of S-SNs; e, FTIR of BSA;f , FTIR of S-SNs; g, UV-vis of S-SNs; h, optical properties of S-SNs).

The S-NSs sensing properties for Fe3+ (a, Sensing ions exploration; b, Fluorescence of sulfur nanosheets was compared with that of quenched iron ions; c, Sensing Fe3+; d, The linearity of sensing the Fe3+ (It is the equilibration time inset Fig. 2d); e, The principle of sensing iron ions).
The S-NSs/Fe3+ sensing properties for SA (a, Sensing SA; b, The linearity of sensing SA; c, The principle of sensing SA).
Effect of different metal ions(a) and SA analogs(b) on S-NSs /Fe3+.
The principle of the S-NSs sensor for Fe3+ and SA.
The difference sensor for Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence sensor based on BSA modified sulfur nanosheets for sequential detection Fe3+ and salicylic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xianyang Normal University, Xianyang 712000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xianyang Normal University, Xianyang 712000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xi’an University of Science and Technology, Xi’an 710054, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xi’an University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xi’an University of Science and Technology, Xi’an 710054, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xi’an Technological University, Xi’an 710021, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xi’an Technological University, Xi’an 710021, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A kind of 2D layered S-NSs were prepared by a simple approach of the direct sonication exfoliation of sublimed sulfur with BSA in an aqueous solution was used as fluorescence sensor for the first time. S-NSs was fabricated for sequential detection of Fe3+ and SA. The linear detection range for Fe3+ and SA was determined as 12–108 μM, and 8–80 μM, respectively. The method include easiness of synthesis of the S-NSs, wide linear range, and environmentally friendly, which makes it an appropriate analytical method for routine applications in biomedical analysis laboratories.

Sulfur nanosheets (S-NSs) have received increasing attention in biochemistry fielded due to adequate inherent antibacterial, antifungal activities, outstanding stability and reusability. The electron transfer between the iron ions (Fe3+) and S-NSs brings S-NSs aggregation lead to the fluorescence intensity of S-NSs decreases in a wide linear concentration range from 12 to 108 μM. More interestingly, the quenched fluorescence of S-NSs/Fe3+ could be recovered by salicylic acid (SA) in a linear range of 8–80 μM because of a stable complex could be form between SA and Fe3+and the proposed sensor for SA showed the outstanding advantages of simplicity, specificity, and fast. The method included environmentally friendly, low-cost, convenient, and wide linear range, which makes it a novel method for routine applications for Fe3+ and SA detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Two-dimensional (2D) layered nanomaterials have elicited ground-breaking research interests in the field of sensor, electronics, energy storage, photocatalysis, optoelectronics and transistors in the past few decades [1–3]. Although 2D materials are easy to synthesize and have good stability, the defects will form a localized state to bind electrons. Therefore, many efforts have been made to overcome the shortcomings of the existing 2D nanomaterials [4–6].

Sulfur is abundant low cost, and environmentally friendly in the presence of the nature, it also has inherent antibacterial and antifungal activities [7–10]. However, sulfur has not received considerable attention from scientists because of the instinctive poor aqueous dispersibility properties until the sulfur quantum dots with good water dispersion are synthesized [11–19]. Shen et al. selected a novel method for preparing layered S-NSs by sonication of sublimed sulfur with bovine serum albumin(BSA), which possess remarkable optical stability [20,21]. The S-NSs obtained using this method showed good stability and aqueous phase dispersion. However, the fluorescence properties of S-NSs have not been explored, the potential superiority of this fresh 2D nanomaterials as sensing materials are highly desired to be explored. Fe3+ion is one of the most widely used metal ions in agricultural and industrial processes and plays an important role in biological systems by binding to different regulatory proteins [22,23]. Thus, the detection of Fe3+ion is of great significance. Currently, various methods for detection Fe3+ have been developed, e.g. atomic absorption spectroscopy, electrochemical, liquid chromatography. However, some of these methods require considerable costs and complex preparation processes [24,25]. Therefore, a convenient, green, sensitive and rapid route for iron ion detection is still challenging. Fluorescence assay was one of the best choices for the detection of iron ion due to its high convenience, real-time monitoring.

SA is a simple small-molecule phenolic compound ubiquitous in plants. It is naturally found in vegetables, water, fruits, and other plants. SA can promote plant rooting, delay fruit maturity and senescence, regulate plant photoperiod, and improve disease resistance. Moreover, SA can dissolve and remove the accumulated thick cuticle and is also used in washing products. However, SA is corrosive; it easily stimulates the skin and mucosa and can react with the proteins in the body. SA was once allowed as a food preservative in many countries. However, studies have shown that eating food containing salicylic acid will stimulate the inner membrane and mucosa of the esophagus and digestive tract, and high SA use will even cause vomiting, abdomen pain, acidosis, and other symptoms [26]. Therefore, this study aims to develop a simple, real-time monitoring, easily available, low-cost, and environmentally friendly and determine SA in biological samples. However, SA detection methods at present are labor-intensive, expensive, and require cumbersome instrumentation.

Here, it was found that Fe3+ ion can quench the fluorescence of S-NSs, accordingly, a simple and convenient analysis method was developed for detection of Fe3+ ion. Moreover, it was also found that SA can recovered the quenched fluorescence of S-NSs-Fe3+ system, accordingly, an “on–off-on”-based S-NS sensor was developed for detection of SA in water in this study (Scheme 1 ). The proposed analysis method shows superb advantage of simpleness, convenience, real-time monitoring, good selectivity and high sensitivity. The electron transfer between the iron ions and the S-NSs brings S-NSs aggregation, leading to the fluorescence intensity decreases by the formation of S-NSs /Fe3+. The quenched fluorescence was then effectively restored by SA due to the formation of a new complex. It was thought that the advantages of the analytical method are mainly attributable to the S-NSs, and the sensing materials of S-NSs itself made a vital role for good specificity, high sensitivity, and excellent anti-interference capability for Fe3+ and SA. This as-constructed sensor exhibited good analytical performance in real water with satisfactory results.

The reagents in this paper were all analytical grade. Standard of SA were procured from Sigma (USA).Poly(ethylene glycol) 400 (PEG-400) was obtained from Beijing Chemical Works. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) of 96 % purity was obtained from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. The distilled water was used during the study.

The morphology of the S-NSs were taken with a JEOL 2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). Florescence spectra were gained by F-7000 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were gained by a UV2100 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a 1 cm path-length quartz cell at room temperature. FTIR were carried out in KBr mode using Thermo Nicolet Avatar 370. Other instruments included ultrasonic cleaners (KQ-300, 40 kHz, Kunshan Ultrasonic Instruments Co. Ltd., China); X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with a using a multifunctional spectrometer (Thermo scientific), the characterization procedures including XPS were carried out by dropping the prepared S-NSs solution on the plate surface of the oxidized silicon as a substrate and drying at 50 °C.

5 mg Of BSA was added to 50 mL of ultrapure water, and 0.50 g sublimed sulfur was added to the BSA solution. The mixture was sonicated for about 72 h. After centrifugation, the supernatant was gained and taken as the S-NSs sample [20]

In this experiment, the exfoliation reagent was BSA because it could bound to the surface of bulk sulfur through polar groups strong and hydrophobic interaction [27]. After sonication, the surface layer of bulk sulfur was adsorbed by BSA, the newly exposed surface is immediately covered by free BSA .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the reaction time of S-NSs with Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction time was determined first to obtain high performance in the fluorescence detection of S-NSs with Fe3+. 1 × 10−3 mol/L Fe3+ was added to S-NSs solution, and the florescence emission spectra of the mixture solution was measured every 2 min after mixing at excitation wavelength of 337 nm with a scanning speed of 1200. The slit width of excitation and emission was 5 nm and was measured every 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions based on S-NSs
ce:section.ce:para: The S-NSs could be used to detect a Fe3+ and the detection process was conducted as follows. Afterward, metal ions (Fe3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Na+, Al3+, Sr2+and Zn2+) were added to 1 × 10−3 mol/L S-NS solution, and the final concentration of each metal ion was 1 × 10−3 mol/L. The solution was then completely mixed and incubated at room temperature for 6 min. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with λ ex = 337 nm and slit width of 5 nm. The sensitivity of Fe3+ detection was analyzed by recording the fluorescence intensity with different Fe3+ concentrations (12–108 μM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of SA based on S-NSs/Fe3+ system
ce:section.ce:para: In the detection of SA, the S-NSs solution containing 1 × 10-3 mol/L Fe3+ and different concentrations of SA and yielded a final volume of 3.0 mL. Fluorescence spectra were gained after 6 min reaction at room temperature.

Hg2+, Cd2+, Na+, Al3+, Sr2+and Zn2+ ions to demonstrate the utility of S-NSs as a fluorescent sensor. Therefore, the ions were added to the test solutions containing 1 × 10−3 mol/L of Fe3+ ions through the standard method. An ion was regarded as interference when its presence contributed to variations in the fluorescence intensity of the sample larger than ± 5 %.

The spike-and-recovery experiment was performed using the standard addition method to evaluate the accuracy of the established method in complex samples. Three different water samples were selected. The water samples were filtered before use. 1 × 10-3mol/L Fe3+ solution (30 μL) was added to 1 × 10-3 mol/L S-NSs solution and calculated the fluorescence intensity change with the distilled water as a standard.

The Fe3+ and SA contents were then determined in accordance with the established method. The spike-and-recovery experiment was conducted. The three samples were detected with the prepared sensor, and the recovery was analyzed.

TEM was widely used to observe the morphology and size of S-NSs. S-NSs samples dispersed in an aqueous medium were completely transparent as seen from the TEM image in Fig. 1 a, indicated they had good dispersibility in water. At the same time, TEM images also revealed that SNSs were stacked layer by layer while some were folded together with curls at the edge. SNSs are predominantly nanosheets. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) showed a distinct crystal form and the nanosheets reach a micron scale in size. The inset in Fig. 1b showed clear diffraction spots of the (044), (026) and (408), further indicating that the synthesized layered sulfur has good crystallization. A transverse layer with a thickness of 6–8 nm can be observed in the AFM sample(Fig. 1c).

The S-NSs composition was verified by XPS, the S-NSs mainly contained S, N, C, and O elements (Fig. 1d). The S elements came from S-NSs, the C, O, and N elements was from BSA, further proved that BSA was covered on the surface of the S-NSs. There were five peaks in the high-resolution XPS S2p spectrum. The peaks at 159.8 and 160.7 eV were attributed to S2− while those at 166.3 and 165.9 eV were assigned to the sulfur of SO3 − (2p2/3) or SO2 − (2p2/3), showing some of the atoms on the sulfur surface are oxidized to sulfonyl/sulfonate [20,28,29].

The characteristic of BSA and S-NSs functional group was employed by FTIR spectroscopy in Fig. 1e and 1f. The amide I at 1636 cm−1 and Ⅱ at 1698-1 of the BSA was very evident in Fig. 1e. There were 2887, 1297, 1248, and 952 cm−1 in S-NSs were obtained in Fig. 1f. The amide I at 1636 cm−1 and Ⅱ at 1698-1 of BSA is still evident. The peaks at 1452 and 999 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibration of C–O–H or C–O–C on the surface of S-NSs. The strong bands at 3232 and 3401 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H and O–H, respectively. The weak characteristic peak at 1637 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C = O. The peak at 1404 cm−1 was related to C = N stretching vibration and –COOH. The band at 1106 cm−1 was assigned to the C-O stretching vibration and 996 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of S-O [30,31]. The results proved there were a large amount of functional groups –OH and –COOH on the surface of S-NSs. This characteristic gives S-NSs good water solubility thus uniformly dispersed in water.

Fluorescence spectroscopy and UV–vis absorption was related to reveal the optical properties of S-NSs (Fig. 1g). There was a slight characteristic peak around 220 nm on absorption spectrum of S-NSs displayed, which should be assigned to n-π* non-bonded electron transitions on the nanosheet surface. The yellow solution of S-NSs showed strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, may be due to quantum size effects, and sp2 domains. When the iron ion is added, the color becomes light; when the SA is added, the blue color becomes blue again under 365 nm UV light. What’s more, the fluorescence spectra of S-NSs displayed a typical excitation-dependent behavior. From in set Fig. 1g, S-NSs was a light yellow solution (Fig. 1g(a)) with bright blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1g(b)). Fig. 1g(c) and Fig. 1g(d) showed the images of S-NSs/Fe3+ and S-NSs/Fe3+/SA, respectively, emitting bright blue fluorescence. Fig. 1g(d) were lighter compared with Fig. 1g(c), further proving that Fe3+ could reduce the fluorescence intensity of S-NSs, resulting in quenching effect. The introduction of SA could effectively recover the fluorescence intensity of S-NSs/Fe3+.

Fig. 1h illustrated the fluorescence spectra of S-NSs with various excitation wavelengths (317–357 nm). With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength was redshifted. The emission intensity varied as excitation wavelengths from 317 nm to 357 nm, the reasons might be attributed to different particle sizes and various surface states [25]. As shown in Fig. 1h, at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity changes under different excitation wavelengths, and the fluorescence intensity is the maximum excitation intensity at 337 nm. The selected excitation wavelength for the next experiment is 337 nm, and the corresponding maximum emission wavelength is 420 nm. The S-NSs have a bright fluorescence [20].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the S-NS sensing properties for Fe3+ and salicylic acid
ce:section.ce:para: The emission spectra of S-NS solution without (blank) and with 1 × 10-3mol/L different metal ions, including Fe3+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Na+, Al3+, Sr2+and Zn2+, are depicted in Fig. 2 a. The figure shows that I0 and I are fluorescence intensities of S-NSs at 420 nm without and with different metal ions, respectively. This finding proved that Fe3+ lead to the strikingly stronger fluorescence quenching effect compared with other ions, a distinct fluorescence quenching to S-NSs in Fig. 2b. Therefore, as a fluorescence sensor, S-NSs have high selectivity to Fe3+ and have the potential to act as fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+. Moreover, except for the selectivity, the selectivity of the sensing materials is also essential to reflect the sensing property of a probe. Under the optimized reaction time of the S-NSs with Fe3+, as shown in the inset of the Fig. 2d, the fluorescence intensity was maintained at 6 min, 6 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time for S-NSs/Fe3+ system. Here, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of S-NSs quenched by Fe3+ was linear with the concentration of Fe3+ in the range from 12 μM to108 μM. A downward trend along with the increase of Fe3+ concentration was shown in Fig. 2c, and the plot of FL intensity change ratio versus Fe3+ concentration (12–108 μM) was shown in Fig. 2d (It is the equilibration time inset Fig. 2d). The regression equation was I = 7765.4–21.16C (μM) with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9960. The cause of the quenching effect probably due to the strong affinity between S-NSs and Fe3+, rapid Fe3+ was chelated to nitrogen and oxygen functional groups on S-NSs surfaces, inducing the electron transfer between them. Fe3+ changes the Zeta potential of S-NSs from − 45.7 mV to − 5.33 mV because of the strong electrostatic attraction between positively charged Fe3+ and negatively charged S-NSs, resulting in the decrease in electrostatic repulsion and the aggregation of S-NSs. The possibility of aggregation-induced emission quenching mechanism because the size of S-NSs was substantially largen when the concentration of Fe3+ was increased (Fig. 2e). Compared with other reported fluorescence sensor for detection of Fe3+ ion, the proposed fluorescence sensor based on S-NSs in this work showed low detection limit and wide linear range, as shown in the Table 1 .

The fluorescence recovery of S-NSs/Fe3+ by SA indicated that S-NSs/Fe3+ could be as a sensor for the detection SA as illustrated in Fig. 3 a. In Fig. 3b, when SA was introduced in S-NSs alone, the fluorescence intensity unchanged, manifesting that SA had no influence on the fluorescence intensity of S-NSs. However, the fluorescence turned on after adding SA to S-NSs/Fe3+ possibly because of a stable complex between SA and Fe3+ was formed. What’s over, the emission intensity of S-NSs/Fe3+ at about 420 nm gradually increased with the SA concentration increased from 8 μM to 80 μM, there was a linear relationship (I = 5140.27 + 41.83C, R2=0.9938) was gained in the range of 8–80 μM. Therefore, a novel sensor with good selectivity and sensitivity for SA could be achieved in accordance with the “on–off-on” system. The principle of the fluorescence reversion is shown in Fig. 3c.

S-NSs containing Fe3+ were supplemented with other metal ions to further study the S-NSs detection properties for Fe3+. Fig. 4 shows that Hg2+ and Ag+ affect this system more than other ions, but the quenching effect of S-NSs/Fe3+ remains unaffected by other metal ions. The selectivity of SA sensor in the presence of co-existing interference compounds such as dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), sulfosalicylic acid (SSA) and uric acid (UA). Fig. 4b showed the fluorescence responses of the sensor to the SA in the absence and presence of 1.0 × 10-3 M of different interferences. The analysis of the sensor for SA was not affected significantly by the interferences, further confirmed the selectivity of the sensor. The selectivity of the metal ions and the analogues of SA were indicated reflected the high selectivity of the sensor. Fe3+ and SA can be determined selectively without interference from coexisting species. In order to the possible actual application in the real sample, the possible metal ion interference was discussed. The tap water, Xianyang lake water, and Qinchi water were tested using the system constructed by S-NSs to prove the feasibility of the new sensing assay constructed by S-NSs in real water samples. The results showed that the interference of common substances was small. The S-NSs/Fe3+ system was determined first. Table 2 shows the effect of the three water samples. However, the effect was insignificant than the two other water samples because the water did not flow and had many impurities. The recovery rate of Fe3+ was 93.0 %–106.0 %, indicating that the method is suitable for the detection of Fe3+ in different water samples and has minimal influence on the experimental results. Table 3 shows the measurement results of S-NSs/Fe3+/SA sensor. The table reveal that the water sample has minimal influence on the sensor and the recovery rate was 86.3 % −100 %, indicating that this method was suitable for the detection of different water samples.

2D-layered S-NSs was prepared by a simple approach sonication exfoliation of sublimed sulfur with BSA. The as-synthesized S-NSs displayed excellent stability and excitation-dependent behavior. The prepared S-NSs was firstly designed as sensing material in fluorescence analysis. The electron transfer between the iron ions and the sulfur nanosheets brings the sulfur nanosheets aggregation lead to the fluorescence intensity decreases in a wide linear concentration range from 12 to 108 μM by the formation of S-NSs /Fe3+. The quenched fluorescence was then restored by SA because of the formation a new complex. Therefore, S-NSs were fabricated for sequential detection of Fe3+ and SA. The linear range for Fe3+ and SA was determined as 12–108 μM and 8–80 μM, respectively. Compared with other analytical methods, this method has the advantages of avoiding cumbersome pretreatment, fast analysis, portability, green, and low cost. Meanwhile, the method can also be used for point-of-time detection of salicylic acid in a minimal setting. However, the presence of interfering substances had a slight effect on the sample determination. The novel sensor provided a sensitive, green, convenient, and rapid route for Fe3+ and SA detection and can be extended to bioanalysis field, and the proposed analysis method based on this fresh 2D-layered S-NSs can also provide reference for other fluorescence analysis and also pave the way for the development of such analytical techniques for drug determination in complicated matrices.
The data sets supporting the results of this article are included within the article and its additional files.
The authors declare that they have no competing financial interests.

Xianyang City Qinchuangyuan science and technology innovation special project(L2022-QCYZX-GY-006); The Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Biological Analysis of the Ministry of Education of Fuzhou University (FS2205).

Shan Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lihua Shen: Methodology. Tao Qiang: Methodology, Conceptualization. Jianyi Xiao: Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Chenyun Cai: Investigation, Formal analysis. Shibo Jia: Methodology, Investigation. Xijing Zhang: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Shaanxi Province Science and Technology Project of China (No.2021JQ-815); Xianyang Qinchuangyuan Science and Technology Innovation Special Project(L2022-QCYZX-GY-006). The Key Laboratory of Food Safety and Biological Analysis of the Ministry of Education of Fuzhou University (FS2205). Blue Talent Project of Xianyang Normal University (XSYQL 202002).
